OpenSpec adds a lightweight spec layer so you agree on what to build before any code is written. 126→ 127→- **Agree before you build** — human and AI align on specs before code gets written 128→- **Stay organized** — each change gets its own folder with proposal, specs, design, and tasks 129→- **Work fluidly** — update any artifact anytime, no rigid phase gates 130→- **Use your tools** — works with 20+ AI assistants via slash commands 131→ 132→### How we compare 133→ 134→**vs. [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)** (GitHub) — Thorough but heavyweight. Rigid phase gates, lots of Markdown, Python setup. OpenSpec is lighter and lets you iterate freely. 135→ 136→**vs. [Kiro](https://kiro.dev)** (AWS) — Powerful but you're locked into their IDE and limited to Claude models. OpenSpec works with the tools you already use. 137→ 138→**vs. nothing** — AI coding without specs means vague prompts and unpredictable results. OpenSpec brings predictability without the ceremony. 139→ 140→## Updating OpenSpec 141→ 142→**Upgrade the package** 143→ 144→```bash 145→npm install -g @fission-ai/openspec@latest 146→``` 147→ 148→**Refresh agent instructions** 149→ 150→Run this inside each project to regenerate AI guidance and ensure the latest slash commands are active: 151→ 152→```bash 153→openspec update 154→``` 155→ 156→## Usage Notes 157→ 158→**Model selection**: OpenSpec works best with high-reasoning models. We recommend Opus 4.5 and GPT 5.2 for both planning and implementation. 159→ 160→**Context hygiene**: OpenSpec benefits from a clean context window. Clear your context before starting implementation and maintain good context hygiene throughout your session. 161→ 162→## Contributing 163→ 164→**Small fixes** — Bug fixes, typo corrections, and minor improvements can be submitted directly as PRs. 165→ 166→**Larger changes** — For new features, significant refactors, or architectural changes, please submit an OpenSpec change proposal first so we can align on intent and goals before implementation begins. 167→ 168→When writing proposals, keep the OpenSpec philosophy in mind: we serve a wide variety of users across different coding agents, models, and use cases. Changes should work well for everyone. 169→ 170→**AI-generated code is welcome** — as long as it's been tested and verified. PRs containing AI-generated code should mention the coding agent and model used (e.g., "Generated with Claude Code using claude-opus-4-5-20251101"). 171→ 172→### Development 173→ 174→- Install dependencies: `pnpm install` 175→- Build: `pnpm run build` 176→- Test: `pnpm test` 177→- Develop CLI locally: `pnpm run dev` or `pnpm run dev:cli` 178→- Conventional commits (one-line): `type(scope): subject` 179→ 180→## Other 181→ 182→

1→name: spec-driven
     2→version: 1
     3→description: Default OpenSpec workflow - proposal → specs → design → tasks
     4→artifacts:
     5→  - id: proposal
     6→    generates: proposal.md
     7→    description: Initial proposal document outlining the change
     8→    template: proposal.md
     9→    instruction: |
    10→      Create the proposal document that establishes WHY this change is needed.
    11→
    12→      Sections:
    13→      - **Why**: 1-2 sentences on the problem or opportunity. What problem does this solve? Why now?
    14→      - **What Changes**: Bullet list of changes. Be specific about new capabilities, modifications, or removals. Mark breaking changes with **BREAKING**.
    15→      - **Capabilities**: Identify which specs will be created or modified:
    16→        - **New Capabilities**: List capabilities being introduced. Each becomes a new `specs/<name>/spec.md`. Use kebab-case names (e.g., `user-auth`, `data-export`).
    17→        - **Modified Capabilities**: List existing capabilities whose REQUIREMENTS are changing. Only include if spec-level behavior changes (not just implementation details). Each needs a delta spec file. Check `openspec/specs/` for existing spec names. Leave empty if no requirement changes.
    18→      - **Impact**: Affected code, APIs, dependencies, or systems.
    19→
    20→      IMPORTANT: The Capabilities section is critical. It creates the contract between
    21→      proposal and specs phases. Research existing specs before filling this in.
    22→      Each capability listed here will need a corresponding spec file.
    23→
    24→      Keep it concise (1-2 pages). Focus on the "why" not the "how" -
    25→      implementation details belong in design.md.
    26→
    27→      This is the foundation - specs, design, and tasks all build on this.
    28→    requires: []
    29→
    30→  - id: specs
    31→    generates: "specs/**/*.md"
    32→    description: Detailed specifications for the change
    33→    template: spec.md
    34→    instruction: |
    35→      Create specification files that define WHAT the system should do.
    36→
    37→      Create one spec file per capability listed in the proposal's Capabilities section.
    38→      - New capabilities: use the exact kebab-case name from the proposal (specs/<capability>/spec.md).
    39→      - Modified capabilities: use the existing spec folder name from openspec/specs/<capability>/ when creating the delta spec at specs/<capability>/spec.md.
    40→
    41→      Delta operations (use ## headers):
    42→      - **ADDED Requirements**: New capabilities
    43→      - **MODIFIED Requirements**: Changed behavior - MUST include full updated content
    44→      - **REMOVED Requirements**: Deprecated features - MUST include **Reason** and **Migration**
    45→      - **RENAMED Requirements**: Name changes only - use FROM:/TO: format
    46→
    47→      Format requirements:
    48→      - Each requirement: `### Requirement: <name>` followed by description
    49→      - Use SHALL/MUST for normative requirements (avoid should/may)
    50→      - Each scenario: `#### Scenario: <name>` with WHEN/THEN format
    51→      - **CRITICAL**: Scenarios MUST use exactly 4 hashtags (`####`). Using 3 hashtags or bullets will fail silently.
    52→      - Every requirement MUST have at least one scenario.
    53→
    54→      MODIFIED requirements workflow:
    55→      1. Locate the existing requirement in openspec/specs/<capability>/spec.md
    56→      2. Copy the ENTIRE requirement block (from `### Requirement:` through all scenarios)
    57→      3. Paste under `## MODIFIED Requirements` and edit to reflect new behavior
    58→      4. Ensure header text matches exactly (whitespace-insensitive)
    59→
    60→      Common pitfall: Using MODIFIED with partial content loses detail at archive time.
    61→      If adding new concerns without changing existing behavior, use ADDED instead.
    62→
    63→      Example:
    64→      ```
    65→      ## ADDED Requirements
    66→
    67→      ### Requirement: User can export data
    68→      The system SHALL allow users to export their data in CSV format.
    69→
    70→      #### Scenario: Successful export
    71→      - **WHEN** user clicks "Export" button
    72→      - **THEN** system downloads a CSV file with all user data
    73→
    74→      ## REMOVED Requirements
    75→
    76→      ### Requirement: Legacy export
    77→      **Reason**: Replaced by new export system
    78→      **Migration**: Use new export endpoint at /api/v2/export
    79→      ```
    80→

    92→      When to include design.md (create only if any apply):
    93→      - Cross-cutting change (multiple services/modules) or new architectural pattern
    94→      - New external dependency or significant data model changes
    95→      - Security, performance, or migration complexity
    96→      - Ambiguity that benefits from technical decisions before coding
    97→
    98→      Sections:
    99→      - **Context**: Background, current state, constraints, stakeholders
   100→      - **Goals / Non-Goals**: What this design achieves and explicitly excludes
   101→      - **Decisions**: Key technical choices with rationale (why X over Y?). Include alternatives considered for each decision.
   102→      - **Risks / Trade-offs**: Known limitations, things that could go wrong. Format: [Risk] → Mitigation
   103→      - **Migration Plan**: Steps to deploy, rollback strategy (if applicable)
   104→      - **Open Questions**: Outstanding decisions or unknowns to resolve
   105→
   106→      Focus on architecture and approach, not line-by-line implementation.
   107→      Reference the proposal for motivation and specs for requirements.
   108→
   109→      Good design docs explain the "why" behind technical decisions.

   116→    template: tasks.md
   117→    instruction: |

   119→
   120→      **IMPORTANT: Follow the template below exactly.** The apply phase parses
   121→      checkbox format to track progress. Tasks not using `- [ ]` won't be tracked.
   122→
   123→      Guidelines:
   124→      - Group related tasks under ## numbered headings
   125→      - Each task MUST be a checkbox: `- [ ] X.Y Task description`
   126→      - Tasks should be small enough to complete in one session
   127→      - Order tasks by dependency (what must be done first?)
   128→
   129→      Example:
   130→      ```
   131→      ## 1. Setup
   132→
   133→      - [ ] 1.1 Create new module structure
   134→      - [ ] 1.2 Add dependencies to package.json
   135→
   136→      ## 2. Core Implementation
   137→
   138→      - [ ] 2.1 Implement data export function
   139→      - [ ] 2.2 Add CSV formatting utilities
   140→      ```
   141→
   142→      Reference specs for what needs to be built, design for how to build it.
   143→      Each task should be verifiable - you know when it's done.
   144→    requires:
   145→      - specs
   146→      - design

   149→  requires: [tasks]
   150→  tracks: tasks.md
   151→  instruction: |

     2→ * Agent Skill Templates
     3→ *

    11→export interface SkillTemplate {
    12→  name: string;

    20→/**
    21→ * Template for openspec-explore skill
    22→ * Explore mode - adaptive thinking partner for exploring ideas and problems
    23→ */
    24→export function getExploreSkillTemplate(): SkillTemplate {
    25→  return {
    26→    name: 'openspec-explore',
    27→    description: 'Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Use when the user wants to think through something before or during a change.',
    28→    instructions: `Enter explore mode. Think deeply. Visualize freely. Follow the conversation wherever it goes.
    29→
    30→**IMPORTANT: Explore mode is for thinking, not implementing.** You may read files, search code, and investigate the codebase, but you must NEVER write code or implement features. If the user asks you to implement something, remind them to exit explore mode first (e.g., start a change with \`/opsx:new\` or \`/opsx:ff\`). You MAY create OpenSpec artifacts (proposals, designs, specs) if the user asks—that's capturing thinking, not implementing.
    31→
    32→**This is a stance, not a workflow.** There are no fixed steps, no required sequence, no mandatory outputs. You're a thinking partner helping the user explore.
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## The Stance
    37→
    38→- **Curious, not prescriptive** - Ask questions that emerge naturally, don't follow a script
    39→- **Open threads, not interrogations** - Surface multiple interesting directions and let the user follow what resonates. Don't funnel them through a single path of questions.
    40→- **Visual** - Use ASCII diagrams liberally when they'd help clarify thinking
    41→- **Adaptive** - Follow interesting threads, pivot when new information emerges
    42→- **Patient** - Don't rush to conclusions, let the shape of the problem emerge
    43→- **Grounded** - Explore the actual codebase when relevant, don't just theorize
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## What You Might Do
    48→
    49→Depending on what the user brings, you might:
    50→
    51→**Explore the problem space**
    52→- Ask clarifying questions that emerge from what they said
    53→- Challenge assumptions
    54→- Reframe the problem
    55→- Find analogies
    56→
    57→**Investigate the codebase**
    58→- Map existing architecture relevant to the discussion
    59→- Find integration points
    60→- Identify patterns already in use
    61→- Surface hidden complexity
    62→
    63→**Compare options**
    64→- Brainstorm multiple approaches
    65→- Build comparison tables
    66→- Sketch tradeoffs
    67→- Recommend a path (if asked)
    68→
    69→**Visualize**
    70→\`\`\`
    71→┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
    72→│     Use ASCII diagrams liberally        │
    73→├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
    74→│                                         │
    75→│   ┌────────┐         ┌────────┐        │
    76→│   │ State  │────────▶│ State  │        │
    77→│   │   A    │         │   B    │        │
    78→│   └────────┘         └────────┘        │
    79→│                                         │
    80→│   System diagrams, state machines,      │
    81→│   data flows, architecture sketches,    │
    82→│   dependency graphs, comparison tables  │
    83→│                                         │
    84→└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
    85→\`\`\`
    86→
    87→**Surface risks and unknowns**
    88→- Identify what could go wrong
    89→- Find gaps in understanding
    90→- Suggest spikes or investigations
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## OpenSpec Awareness
    95→
    96→You have full context of the OpenSpec system. Use it naturally, don't force it.
    97→
    98→### Check for context
    99→
   100→At the start, quickly check what exists:
   101→\`\`\`bash
   102→openspec list --json
   103→\`\`\`
   104→
   105→This tells you:
   106→- If there are active changes
   107→- Their names, schemas, and status
   108→- What the user might be working on
   109→
   110→### When no change exists
   111→
   112→Think freely. When insights crystallize, you might offer:
   113→
   114→- "This feels solid enough to start a change. Want me to create one?"
   115→  → Can transition to \`/opsx:new\` or \`/opsx:ff\`
   116→- Or keep exploring - no pressure to formalize
   117→
   118→### When a change exists
   119→
   120→If the user mentions a change or you detect one is relevant:
   121→
   122→1. **Read existing artifacts for context**
   123→   - \`openspec/changes/<name>/proposal.md\`
   124→   - \`openspec/changes/<name>/design.md\`
   125→   - \`openspec/changes/<name>/tasks.md\`
   126→   - etc.
   127→
   128→2. **Reference them naturally in conversation**
   129→   - "Your design mentions using Redis, but we just realized SQLite fits better..."
   130→   - "The proposal scopes this to premium users, but we're now thinking everyone..."
   131→
   132→3. **Offer to capture when decisions are made**
   133→
   134→   | Insight Type | Where to Capture |
   135→   |--------------|------------------|
   136→   | New requirement discovered | \`specs/<capability>/spec.md\` |
   137→   | Requirement changed | \`specs/<capability>/spec.md\` |
   138→   | Design decision made | \`design.md\` |
   139→   | Scope changed | \`proposal.md\` |
   140→   | New work identified | \`tasks.md\` |
   141→   | Assumption invalidated | Relevant artifact |
   142→
   143→   Example offers:
   144→   - "That's a design decision. Capture it in design.md?"
   145→   - "This is a new requirement. Add it to specs?"
   146→   - "This changes scope. Update the proposal?"
   147→
   148→4. **The user decides** - Offer and move on. Don't pressure. Don't auto-capture.
   149→
   150→---
   151→
   152→## What You Don't Have To Do
   153→
   154→- Follow a script
   155→- Ask the same questions every time
   156→- Produce a specific artifact
   157→- Reach a conclusion
   158→- Stay on topic if a tangent is valuable
   159→- Be brief (this is thinking time)
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## Handling Different Entry Points
   164→
   165→**User brings a vague idea:**
   166→\`\`\`
   167→User: I'm thinking about adding real-time collaboration
   168→
   169→You: Real-time collab is a big space. Let me think about this...
   170→
   171→      COLLABORATION SPECTRUM
   172→      ════════════════════════════════════════════
   173→
   174→      Awareness          Coordination         Sync
   175→          │                   │                 │
   176→          ▼                   ▼                 ▼
   177→      ┌────────┐         ┌────────┐        ┌────────┐
   178→      │Presence│         │Cursors │        │  CRDT  │
   179→      │  "3    │         │ Multi  │        │Conflict│
   180→      │online" │         │ select │        │  free  │
   181→      └────────┘         └────────┘        └────────┘
   182→          │                   │                 │
   183→       trivial            moderate           complex
   184→
   185→      Where's your head at?
   186→\`\`\`
   187→
   188→**User brings a specific problem:**
   189→\`\`\`
   190→User: The auth system is a mess
   191→
   192→You: [reads codebase]
   193→
   194→     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   195→     │              CURRENT AUTH FLOW              │
   196→     └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   197→                        │
   198→          ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
   199→          ▼             ▼             ▼
   200→     ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐

1→# Concepts
     2→
     3→This guide explains the core ideas behind OpenSpec and how they fit together. For practical usage, see [Getting Started](getting-started.md) and [Workflows](workflows.md).
     4→
     5→## Philosophy
     6→
     7→OpenSpec is built around four principles:
     8→
     9→```
    10→fluid not rigid       — no phase gates, work on what makes sense
    11→iterative not waterfall — learn as you build, refine as you go
    12→easy not complex      — lightweight setup, minimal ceremony
    13→brownfield-first      — works with existing codebases, not just greenfield
    14→```
    15→
    16→### Why These Principles Matter
    17→
    18→**Fluid not rigid.** Traditional spec systems lock you into phases: first you plan, then you implement, then you're done. OpenSpec is more flexible — you can create artifacts in any order that makes sense for your work.
    19→
    20→**Iterative not waterfall.** Requirements change. Understanding deepens. What seemed like a good approach at the start might not hold up after you see the codebase. OpenSpec embraces this reality.
    21→
    22→**Easy not complex.** Some spec frameworks require extensive setup, rigid formats, or heavyweight processes. OpenSpec stays out of your way. Initialize in seconds, start working immediately, customize only if you need to.
    23→
    24→**Brownfield-first.** Most software work isn't building from scratch — it's modifying existing systems. OpenSpec's delta-based approach makes it easy to specify changes to existing behavior, not just describe new systems.
    25→
    26→## The Big Picture
    27→
    28→OpenSpec organizes your work into two main areas:
    29→
    30→```
    31→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    32→│                        openspec/                                 │
    33→│                                                                  │
    34→│   ┌─────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │
    35→│   │       specs/        │      │         changes/              │ │
    36→│   │                     │      │                               │ │
    37→│   │  Source of truth    │◄─────│  Proposed modifications       │ │
    38→│   │  How your system    │ merge│  Each change = one folder     │ │
    39→│   │  currently works    │      │  Contains artifacts + deltas  │ │
    40→│   │                     │      │                               │ │
    41→│   └─────────────────────┘      └──────────────────────────────┘ │
    42→│                                                                  │
    43→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    44→```
    45→
    46→**Specs** are the source of truth — they describe how your system currently behaves.
    47→
    48→**Changes** are proposed modifications — they live in separate folders until you're ready to merge them.
    49→
    50→This separation is key. You can work on multiple changes in parallel without conflicts. You can review a change before it affects the main specs. And when you archive a change, its deltas merge cleanly into the source of truth.
    51→
    52→## Specs
    53→
    54→Specs describe your system's behavior using structured requirements and scenarios.
    55→
    56→### Structure
    57→
    58→```
    59→openspec/specs/
    60→├── auth/
    61→│   └── spec.md           # Authentication behavior
    62→├── payments/
    63→│   └── spec.md           # Payment processing
    64→├── notifications/
    65→│   └── spec.md           # Notification system
    66→└── ui/
    67→    └── spec.md           # UI behavior and themes
    68→```
    69→
    70→Organize specs by domain — logical groupings that make sense for your system. Common patterns:
    71→
    72→- **By feature area**: `auth/`, `payments/`, `search/`
    73→- **By component**: `api/`, `frontend/`, `workers/`
    74→- **By bounded context**: `ordering/`, `fulfillment/`, `inventory/`
    75→
    76→### Spec Format
    77→
    78→A spec contains requirements, and each requirement has scenarios:
    79→
    80→```markdown
    81→# Auth Specification
    82→
    83→## Purpose
    84→Authentication and session management for the application.
    85→
    86→## Requirements
    87→
    88→### Requirement: User Authentication
    89→The system SHALL issue a JWT token upon successful login.
    90→
    91→#### Scenario: Valid credentials
    92→- GIVEN a user with valid credentials
    93→- WHEN the user submits login form
    94→- THEN a JWT token is returned
    95→- AND the user is redirected to dashboard
    96→
    97→#### Scenario: Invalid credentials
    98→- GIVEN invalid credentials
    99→- WHEN the user submits login form
   100→- THEN an error message is displayed
   101→- AND no token is issued
   102→
   103→### Requirement: Session Expiration
   104→The system MUST expire sessions after 30 minutes of inactivity.
   105→
   106→#### Scenario: Idle timeout
   107→- GIVEN an authenticated session
   108→- WHEN 30 minutes pass without activity
   109→- THEN the session is invalidated
   110→- AND the user must re-authenticate
   111→```
   112→
   113→**Key elements:**
   114→
   115→| Element | Purpose |
   116→|---------|---------|
   117→| `## Purpose` | High-level description of this spec's domain |
   118→| `### Requirement:` | A specific behavior the system must have |
   119→| `#### Scenario:` | A concrete example of the requirement in action |
   120→| SHALL/MUST/SHOULD | RFC 2119 keywords indicating requirement strength |
   121→
   122→### Why Structure Specs This Way
   123→
   124→**Requirements are the "what"** — they state what the system should do without specifying implementation.
   125→
   126→**Scenarios are the "when"** — they provide concrete examples that can be verified. Good scenarios:
   127→- Are testable (you could write an automated test for them)
   128→- Cover both happy path and edge cases
   129→- Use Given/When/Then or similar structured format
   130→
   131→**RFC 2119 keywords** (SHALL, MUST, SHOULD, MAY) communicate intent:
   132→- **MUST/SHALL** — absolute requirement
   133→- **SHOULD** — recommended, but exceptions exist
   134→- **MAY** — optional
   135→
   136→## Changes
   137→
   138→A change is a proposed modification to your system, packaged as a folder with everything needed to understand and implement it.
   139→
   140→### Change Structure
   141→
   142→```
   143→openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/
   144→├── proposal.md           # Why and what
   145→├── design.md             # How (technical approach)
   146→├── tasks.md              # Implementation checklist
   147→├── .openspec.yaml        # Change metadata (optional)
   148→└── specs/                # Delta specs
   149→    └── ui/
   150→        └── spec.md       # What's changing in ui/spec.md
   151→```
   152→
   153→Each change is self-contained. It has:
   154→- **Artifacts** — documents that capture intent, design, and tasks
   155→- **Delta specs** — specifications for what's being added, modified, or removed
   156→- **Metadata** — optional configuration for this specific change
   157→
   158→### Why Changes Are Folders
   159→
   160→Packaging a change as a folder has several benefits:
   161→
   162→1. **Everything together.** Proposal, design, tasks, and specs live in one place. No hunting through different locations.
   163→
   164→2. **Parallel work.** Multiple changes can exist simultaneously without conflicting. Work on `add-dark-mode` while `fix-auth-bug` is also in progress.
   165→
   166→3. **Clean history.** When archived, changes move to `changes/archive/` with their full context preserved. You can look back and understand not just what changed, but why.
   167→
   168→4. **Review-friendly.** A change folder is easy to review — open it, read the proposal, check the design, see the spec deltas.
   169→
   170→## Artifacts
   171→
   172→Artifacts are the documents within a change that guide the work.
   173→
   174→### The Artifact Flow
   175→
   176→```
   177→proposal ──────► specs ──────► design ──────► tasks ──────► implement
   178→    │               │             │              │
   179→   why            what           how          steps
   180→ + scope        changes       approach      to take
   181→```
   182→
   183→Artifacts build on each other. Each artifact provides context for the next.
   184→
   185→### Artifact Types
   186→
   187→#### Proposal (`proposal.md`)
   188→
   189→The proposal captures **intent**, **scope**, and **approach** at a high level.
   190→
   191→```markdown
   192→# Proposal: Add Dark Mode
   193→
   194→## Intent
   195→Users have requested a dark mode option to reduce eye strain
   196→during nighttime usage and match system preferences.
   197→
   198→## Scope
   199→In scope:
   200→- Theme toggle in settings
   201→- System preference detection
   202→- Persist preference in localStorage
   203→
   204→Out of scope:
   205→- Custom color themes (future work)
   206→- Per-page theme overrides
   207→
   208→## Approach
   209→Use CSS custom properties for theming with a React context
   210→for state management. Detect system preference on first load,
   211→allow manual override.
   212→```
   213→
   214→**When to update the proposal:**
   215→- Scope changes (narrowing or expanding)
   216→- Intent clarifies (better understanding of the problem)
   217→- Approach fundamentally shifts
   218→
   219→#### Specs (delta specs in `specs/`)
   220→
   221→Delta specs describe **what's changing** relative to the current specs. See [Delta Specs](#delta-specs) below.
   222→
   223→#### Design (`design.md`)
   224→
   225→The design captures **technical approach** and **architecture decisions**.
   226→
   227→```markdown
   228→# Design: Add Dark Mode
   229→
   230→## Technical Approach
   231→Theme state managed via React Context to avoid prop drilling.
   232→CSS custom properties enable runtime switching without class toggling.
   233→
   234→## Architecture Decisions
   235→
   236→### Decision: Context over Redux
   237→Using React Context for theme state because:
   238→- Simple binary state (light/dark)
   239→- No complex state transitions
   240→- Avoids adding Redux dependency
   241→
   242→### Decision: CSS Custom Properties
   243→Using CSS variables instead of CSS-in-JS because:
   244→- Works with existing stylesheet
   245→- No runtime overhead
   246→- Browser-native solution
   247→
   248→## Data Flow
   249→```
   250→ThemeProvider (context)
   251→       │
   252→       ▼
   253→ThemeToggle ◄──► localStorage
   254→       │
   255→       ▼
   256→CSS Variables (applied to :root)
   257→```
   258→
   259→## File Changes
   260→- `src/contexts/ThemeContext.tsx` (new)
   261→- `src/components/ThemeToggle.tsx` (new)
   262→- `src/styles/globals.css` (modified)
   263→```
   264→
   265→**When to update the design:**
   266→- Implementation reveals the approach won't work
   267→- Better solution discovered
   268→- Dependencies or constraints change
   269→
   270→#### Tasks (`tasks.md`)
   271→
   272→Tasks are the **implementation checklist** — concrete steps with checkboxes.
   273→
   274→```markdown
   275→# Tasks
   276→
   277→## 1. Theme Infrastructure
   278→- [ ] 1.1 Create ThemeContext with light/dark state
   279→- [ ] 1.2 Add CSS custom properties for colors
   280→- [ ] 1.3 Implement localStorage persistence
   281→- [ ] 1.4 Add system preference detection
   282→
   283→## 2. UI Components
   284→- [ ] 2.1 Create ThemeToggle component
   285→- [ ] 2.2 Add toggle to settings page
   286→- [ ] 2.3 Update Header to include quick toggle
   287→
   288→## 3. Styling
   289→- [ ] 3.1 Define dark theme color palette
   290→- [ ] 3.2 Update components to use CSS variables
   291→- [ ] 3.3 Test contrast ratios for accessibility
   292→```
   293→
   294→**Task best practices:**
   295→- Group related tasks under headings
   296→- Use hierarchical numbering (1.1, 1.2, etc.)
   297→- Keep tasks small enough to complete in one session
   298→- Check tasks off as you complete them
   299→
   300→## Delta Specs
   301→
   302→Delta specs are the key concept that makes OpenSpec work for brownfield development. They describe **what's changing** rather than restating the entire spec.
   303→
   304→### The Format
   305→
   306→```markdown
   307→# Delta for Auth
   308→
   309→## ADDED Requirements
   310→
   311→### Requirement: Two-Factor Authentication
   312→The system MUST support TOTP-based two-factor authentication.
   313→
   314→#### Scenario: 2FA enrollment
   315→- GIVEN a user without 2FA enabled
   316→- WHEN the user enables 2FA in settings
   317→- THEN a QR code is displayed for authenticator app setup
   318→- AND the user must verify with a code before activation
   319→
   320→#### Scenario: 2FA login
   321→- GIVEN a user with 2FA enabled
   322→- WHEN the user submits valid credentials
   323→- THEN an OTP challenge is presented
   324→- AND login completes only after valid OTP
   325→
   326→## MODIFIED Requirements
   327→
   328→### Requirement: Session Expiration
   329→The system MUST expire sessions after 15 minutes of inactivity.
   330→(Previously: 30 minutes)
   331→
   332→#### Scenario: Idle timeout
   333→- GIVEN an authenticated session
   334→- WHEN 15 minutes pass without activity
   335→- THEN the session is invalidated
   336→
   337→## REMOVED Requirements
   338→
   339→### Requirement: Remember Me
   340→(Deprecated in favor of 2FA. Users should re-authenticate each session.)
   341→```
   342→
   343→### Delta Sections
   344→
   345→| Section | Meaning | What Happens on Archive |
   346→|---------|---------|------------------------|
   347→| `## ADDED Requirements` | New behavior | Appended to main spec |
   348→| `## MODIFIED Requirements` | Changed behavior | Replaces existing requirement |
   349→| `## REMOVED Requirements` | Deprecated behavior | Deleted from main spec |
   350→
   351→### Why Deltas Instead of Full Specs
   352→
   353→**Clarity.** A delta shows exactly what's changing. Reading a full spec, you'd have to diff it mentally against the current version.
   354→
   355→**Conflict avoidance.** Two changes can touch the same spec file without conflicting, as long as they modify different requirements.
   356→
   357→**Review efficiency.** Reviewers see the change, not the unchanged context. Focus on what matters.
   358→
   359→**Brownfield fit.** Most work modifies existing behavior. Deltas make modifications first-class, not an afterthought.
   360→
   361→## Schemas
   362→
   363→Schemas define the artifact types and their dependencies for a workflow.
   364→
   365→### How Schemas Work
   366→
   367→```yaml
   368→# openspec/schemas/spec-driven/schema.yaml
   369→name: spec-driven
   370→artifacts:
   371→  - id: proposal
   372→    generates: proposal.md
   373→    requires: []              # No dependencies, can create first
   374→
   375→  - id: specs
   376→    generates: specs/**/*.md
   377→    requires: [proposal]      # Needs proposal before creating
   378→
   379→  - id: design
   380→    generates: design.md
   381→    requires: [proposal]      # Can create in parallel with specs
   382→
   383→  - id: tasks
   384→    generates: tasks.md
   385→    requires: [specs, design] # Needs both specs and design first
   386→```
   387→
   388→**Artifacts form a dependency graph:**
   389→
   390→```
   391→                    proposal
   392→                   (root node)
   393→                       │
   394→         ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
   395→         │                           │
   396→         ▼                           ▼
   397→      specs                       design
   398→   (requires:                  (requires:
   399→    proposal)                   proposal)
   400→         │                           │
   401→         └─────────────┬─────────────┘
   402→                       │
   403→                       ▼
   404→                    tasks
   405→                (requires:
   406→                specs, design)
   407→```
   408→
   409→**Dependencies are enablers, not gates.** They show what's possible to create, not what you must create next. You can skip design if you don't need it. You can create specs before or after design — both depend only on proposal.
   410→
   411→### Built-in Schemas
   412→
   413→**spec-driven** (default)
   414→
   415→The standard workflow for spec-driven development:
   416→
   417→```
   418→proposal → specs → design → tasks → implement
   419→```
   420→
   421→Best for: Most feature work where you want to agree on specs before implementation.
   422→
   423→### Custom Schemas
   424→
   425→Create custom schemas for your team's workflow:
   426→
   427→```bash
   428→# Create from scratch
   429→openspec schema init research-first
   430→
   431→# Or fork an existing one
   432→openspec schema fork spec-driven research-first
   433→```
   434→
   435→**Example custom schema:**
   436→
   437→```yaml
   438→# openspec/schemas/research-first/schema.yaml
   439→name: research-first
   440→artifacts:
   441→  - id: research
   442→    generates: research.md
   443→    requires: []           # Do research first
   444→
   445→  - id: proposal
   446→    generates: proposal.md
   447→    requires: [research]   # Proposal informed by research
   448→
   449→  - id: tasks
   450→    generates: tasks.md
   451→    requires: [proposal]   # Skip specs/design, go straight to tasks
   452→```
   453→
   454→See [Customization](customization.md) for full details on creating and using custom schemas.
   455→
   456→## Archive
   457→
   458→Archiving completes a change by merging its delta specs into the main specs and preserving the change for history.
   459→
   460→### What Happens When You Archive
   461→
   462→```
   463→Before archive:
   464→
   465→openspec/
   466→├── specs/
   467→│   └── auth/
   468→│       └── spec.md ◄────────────────┐
   469→└── changes/                         │
   470→    └── add-2fa/                     │
   471→        ├── proposal.md              │
   472→        ├── design.md                │ merge
   473→        ├── tasks.md                 │
   474→        └── specs/                   │
   475→            └── auth/                │
   476→                └── spec.md ─────────┘
   477→
   478→
   479→After archive:
   480→
   481→openspec/
   482→├── specs/
   483→│   └── auth/
   484→│       └── spec.md        # Now includes 2FA requirements
   485→└── changes/
   486→    └── archive/
   487→        └── 2025-01-24-add-2fa/    # Preserved for history
   488→            ├── proposal.md
   489→            ├── design.md
   490→            ├── tasks.md
   491→            └── specs/
   492→                └── auth/
   493→                    └── spec.md
   494→```
   495→
   496→### The Archive Process
   497→
   498→1. **Merge deltas.** Each delta spec section (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED) is applied to the corresponding main spec.
   499→
   500→2. **Move to archive.** The change folder moves to `changes/archive/` with a date prefix for chronological ordering.
   501→
   502→3. **Preserve context.** All artifacts remain intact in the archive. You can always look back to understand why a change was made.
   503→
   504→### Why Archive Matters
   505→
   506→**Clean state.** Active changes (`changes/`) shows only work in progress. Completed work moves out of the way.
   507→
   508→**Audit trail.** The archive preserves the full context of every change — not just what changed, but the proposal explaining why, the design explaining how, and the tasks showing the work done.
   509→
   510→**Spec evolution.** Specs grow organically as changes are archived. Each archive merges its deltas, building up a comprehensive specification over time.
   511→
   512→## How It All Fits Together
   513→
   514→```
   515→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   516→│                              OPENSPEC FLOW                                   │
   517→│                                                                              │
   518→│   ┌────────────────┐                                                         │
   519→│   │  1. START      │  /opsx:new creates a change folder                      │
   520→│   │     CHANGE     │                                                         │
   521→│   └───────┬────────┘                                                         │
   522→│           │                                                                  │
   523→│           ▼                                                                  │
   524→│   ┌────────────────┐                                                         │
   525→│   │  2. CREATE     │  /opsx:ff or /opsx:continue                             │
   526→│   │     ARTIFACTS  │  Creates proposal → specs → design → tasks              │
   527→│   │                │  (based on schema dependencies)                         │
   528→│   └───────┬────────┘                                                         │
   529→│           │                                                                  │
   530→│           ▼                                                                  │
   531→│   ┌────────────────┐                                                         │
   532→│   │  3. IMPLEMENT  │  /opsx:apply                                            │
   533→│   │     TASKS      │  Work through tasks, checking them off                  │
   534→│   │                │◄──── Update artifacts as you learn                      │
   535→│   └───────┬────────┘                                                         │
   536→│           │                                                                  │
   537→│           ▼                                                                  │
   538→│   ┌────────────────┐                                                         │
   539→│   │  4. VERIFY     │  /opsx:verify (optional)                                │
   540→│   │     WORK       │  Check implementation matches specs                     │
   541→│   └───────┬────────┘                                                         │
   542→│           │                                                                  │
   543→│           ▼                                                                  │
   544→│   ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
   545→│   │  5. ARCHIVE    │────►│  Delta specs merge into main specs           │   │
   546→│   │     CHANGE     │     │  Change folder moves to archive/             │   │
   547→│   └────────────────┘     │  Specs are now the updated source of truth   │   │
   548→│                          └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
   549→│                                                                              │
   550→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   551→```
   552→
   553→**The virtuous cycle:**
   554→
   555→1. Specs describe current behavior
   556→2. Changes propose modifications (as deltas)
   557→3. Implementation makes the changes real
   558→4. Archive merges deltas into specs
   559→5. Specs now describe the new behavior
   560→6. Next change builds on updated specs
   561→
   562→## Glossary
   563→
   564→| Term | Definition |
   565→|------|------------|
   566→| **Artifact** | A document within a change (proposal, design, tasks, or delta specs) |
   567→| **Archive** | The process of completing a change and merging its deltas into main specs |
   568→| **Change** | A proposed modification to the system, packaged as a folder with artifacts |
   569→| **Delta spec** | A spec that describes changes (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED) relative to current specs |
   570→| **Domain** | A logical grouping for specs (e.g., `auth/`, `payments/`) |
   571→| **Requirement** | A specific behavior the system must have |
   572→| **Scenario** | A concrete example of a requirement, typically in Given/When/Then format |
   573→| **Schema** | A definition of artifact types and their dependencies |
   574→| **Spec** | A specification describing system behavior, containing requirements and scenarios |
   575→| **Source of truth** | The `openspec/specs/` directory, containing the current agreed-upon behavior |
   576→

   578→

   580→- [Workflows](workflows.md) - Common patterns and when to use each
   581→- [Commands](commands.md) - Full command reference

1→# Workflows
     2→
     3→This guide covers common workflow patterns for OpenSpec and when to use each one. For basic setup, see [Getting Started](getting-started.md). For command reference, see [Commands](commands.md).
     4→
     5→## Philosophy: Actions, Not Phases
     6→
     7→Traditional workflows force you through phases: planning, then implementation, then done. But real work doesn't fit neatly into boxes.
     8→
     9→OPSX takes a different approach:
    10→
    11→```text
    12→Traditional (phase-locked):
    13→
    14→  PLANNING ────────► IMPLEMENTING ────────► DONE
    15→      │                    │
    16→      │   "Can't go back"  │
    17→      └────────────────────┘
    18→
    19→OPSX (fluid actions):
    20→
    21→  proposal ──► specs ──► design ──► tasks ──► implement
    22→```
    23→
    24→**Key principles:**
    25→
    26→- **Actions, not phases** - Commands are things you can do, not stages you're stuck in
    27→- **Dependencies are enablers** - They show what's possible, not what's required next
    28→
    29→> **Customization:** OPSX workflows are driven by schemas that define artifact sequences. See [Customization](customization.md) for details on creating custom schemas.
    30→
    31→## Workflow Patterns
    32→
    33→### Quick Feature
    34→
    35→When you know what you want to build and just need to execute:
    36→
    37→```text
    38→/opsx:new ──► /opsx:ff ──► /opsx:apply ──► /opsx:verify ──► /opsx:archive
    39→```
    40→
    41→**Example conversation:**
    42→
    43→```text
    44→You: /opsx:new add-logout-button
    45→
    46→AI:  Created openspec/changes/add-logout-button/
    47→     Ready to create: proposal
    48→
    49→You: /opsx:ff
    50→
    51→AI:  Creating all planning artifacts...
    52→     ✓ proposal.md
    53→     ✓ specs/
    54→     ✓ design.md
    55→     ✓ tasks.md
    56→     Ready for implementation!
    57→
    58→You: /opsx:apply
    59→
    60→AI:  Working through tasks...
    61→     ✓ Added logout button to header
    62→     ✓ Implemented session clearing
    63→     ✓ Added confirmation dialog
    64→     All tasks complete!
    65→
    66→You: /opsx:archive
    67→
    68→AI:  ✓ Merged specs
    69→     ✓ Archived change
    70→```
    71→
    72→**Best for:** Small to medium features, bug fixes, straightforward changes.
    73→
    74→### Exploratory
    75→
    76→When requirements are unclear or you need to investigate first:
    77→
    78→```text
    79→/opsx:explore ──► /opsx:new ──► /opsx:continue ──► ... ──► /opsx:apply
    80→```
    81→
    82→**Example conversation:**
    83→
    84→```text
    85→You: /opsx:explore
    86→
    87→AI:  What would you like to explore?
    88→
    89→You: I want to improve page load performance but I'm not sure
    90→     where the bottlenecks are.
    91→
    92→AI:  Let me help investigate...
    93→     [Analyzes bundle sizes, identifies slow queries, reviews
    94→     component render patterns]
    95→
    96→     I found three main bottlenecks:
    97→     1. Large unoptimized images
    98→     2. Synchronous data fetching in ProductList
    99→     3. Re-renders from context changes
   100→
   101→     Which would you like to address first?
   102→
   103→You: Let's tackle the data fetching issue.
   104→
   105→You: /opsx:new optimize-product-list-fetching
   106→
   107→AI:  Created change. Ready to create: proposal
   108→
   109→You: /opsx:continue
   110→
   111→AI:  Creating proposal based on our exploration...
   112→     [Writes proposal incorporating investigation insights]
   113→
   114→     Ready to create: specs, design
   115→     Which would you like next?
   116→
   117→You: /opsx:continue
   118→
   119→AI:  Creating specs...
   120→```
   121→
   122→**Best for:** Performance optimization, debugging, architectural decisions, unclear requirements.
   123→
   124→### Parallel Changes
   125→
   126→Work on multiple changes at once:
   127→
   128→```text
   129→Change A: /opsx:new ──► /opsx:ff ──► /opsx:apply (in progress)
   130→                                         │
   131→                                    context switch
   132→                                         │
   133→Change B: /opsx:new ──► /opsx:ff ──────► /opsx:apply
   134→```
   135→
   136→**Example conversation:**
   137→
   138→```text
   139→You: I'm working on add-dark-mode but got pulled into a bug fix
   140→
   141→You: /opsx:new fix-login-redirect
   142→
   143→AI:  Created openspec/changes/fix-login-redirect/
   144→
   145→You: /opsx:ff
   146→
   147→AI:  ✓ Created planning artifacts
   148→
   149→You: /opsx:apply
   150→
   151→AI:  Working on fix-login-redirect...
   152→     [Implements the fix]
   153→     All tasks complete!
   154→
   155→You: /opsx:archive
   156→
   157→AI:  ✓ Archived fix-login-redirect
   158→
   159→You: Let's get back to dark mode
   160→
   161→You: /opsx:apply add-dark-mode
   162→
   163→AI:  Resuming add-dark-mode...
   164→     Picking up at task 2.3: Update Header...
   165→```
   166→
   167→**Best for:** Parallel work streams, urgent interrupts, team collaboration.
   168→
   169→When you have multiple completed changes, use `/opsx:bulk-archive`:
   170→
   171→```text
   172→You: /opsx:bulk-archive
   173→
   174→AI:  Found 3 completed changes:
   175→     - add-dark-mode (all tasks done)
   176→     - fix-login-redirect (all tasks done)
   177→     - update-footer (all tasks done)
   178→
   179→     Checking for spec conflicts...
   180→     ⚠ add-dark-mode and update-footer both touch specs/ui/
   181→
   182→     Inspecting codebase to resolve...
   183→     Both changes are implemented. Will apply in chronological order.
   184→
   185→     Archive all 3 changes?
   186→
   187→You: Yes
   188→
   189→AI:  ✓ Archived add-dark-mode
   190→     ✓ Archived fix-login-redirect
   191→     ✓ Archived update-footer
   192→     Specs merged in order: add-dark-mode → update-footer
   193→```
   194→
   195→Bulk archive detects when multiple changes touch the same specs and resolves conflicts by checking what's actually implemented.
   196→
   197→### Completing a Change
   198→
   199→The recommended completion flow:
   200→
   201→```text
   202→/opsx:apply ──► /opsx:verify ──► /opsx:archive
   203→                    │                 │
   204→              validates          prompts to sync
   205→              implementation     if needed
   206→```
   207→
   208→#### Verify: Check Your Work
   209→
   210→`/opsx:verify` validates implementation against your artifacts across three dimensions:
   211→
   212→```text
   213→You: /opsx:verify
   214→
   215→AI:  Verifying add-auth...
   216→
   217→     COMPLETENESS
   218→     ✓ All 12 tasks in tasks.md are checked
   219→     ✓ All requirements in specs have corresponding code
   220→     ⚠ Scenario "Session timeout after inactivity" not tested
   221→
   222→     CORRECTNESS
   223→     ✓ Implementation matches spec intent
   224→     ✓ Edge cases from scenarios are handled
   225→     ✓ Error states match spec definitions
   226→
   227→     COHERENCE
   228→     ✓ Design decisions reflected in code structure
   229→     ✓ Naming conventions consistent with design.md
   230→     ⚠ Design mentions "event-driven" but implementation uses polling
   231→
   232→     SUMMARY
   233→     ─────────────────────────────
   234→     Critical issues: 0
   235→     Warnings: 2
   236→     Ready to archive: Yes (with warnings)
   237→
   238→     Recommendations:
   239→     1. Add test for session timeout scenario
   240→     2. Consider refactoring to event-driven as designed, or update design.md
   241→```
   242→
   243→**What verify checks:**
   244→
   245→| Dimension | What it validates |
   246→|-----------|------------------|
   247→| Completeness | All tasks done, all requirements implemented, scenarios covered |
   248→| Correctness | Implementation matches spec intent, edge cases handled |
   249→| Coherence | Design decisions reflected in code, patterns consistent |
   250→
   251→Verify won't block archive, but it surfaces issues you might want to address first.
   252→
   253→#### Archive: Finalize the Change
   254→
   255→`/opsx:archive` completes the change and moves it to the archive:
   256→
   257→```text
   258→You: /opsx:archive
   259→
   260→AI:  Archiving add-auth...
   261→
   262→     Artifact status:
   263→     ✓ proposal.md exists
   264→     ✓ specs/ exists
   265→     ✓ design.md exists
   266→     ✓ tasks.md exists (12/12 tasks complete)
   267→
   268→     Delta specs: Not yet synced to main
   269→     → Would you like to sync now?
   270→
   271→You: Yes
   272→
   273→AI:  ✓ Synced specs to openspec/specs/auth/spec.md
   274→     ✓ Moved to openspec/changes/archive/2025-01-24-add-auth/
   275→
   276→     Change archived successfully.
   277→```
   278→
   279→Archive will prompt if specs aren't synced. It won't block on incomplete tasks, but it will warn you.
   280→
   281→## When to Use What
   282→
   283→### `/opsx:ff` vs `/opsx:continue`
   284→
   285→| Situation | Use |
   286→|-----------|-----|
   287→| Clear requirements, ready to build | `/opsx:ff` |
   288→| Exploring, want to review each step | `/opsx:continue` |
   289→| Want to iterate on proposal before specs | `/opsx:continue` |
   290→| Time pressure, need to move fast | `/opsx:ff` |
   291→| Complex change, want control | `/opsx:continue` |
   292→
   293→**Rule of thumb:** If you can describe the full scope upfront, use `/opsx:ff`. If you're figuring it out as you go, use `/opsx:continue`.
   294→
   295→### When to Update vs Start Fresh
   296→
   297→A common question: when is updating an existing change okay, and when should you start a new one?
   298→
   299→**Update the existing change when:**
   300→
   301→- Same intent, refined execution
   302→- Scope narrows (MVP first, rest later)
   303→- Learning-driven corrections (codebase isn't what you expected)
   304→- Design tweaks based on implementation discoveries
   305→
   306→**Start a new change when:**
   307→
   308→- Intent fundamentally changed
   309→- Scope exploded to different work entirely
   310→- Original change can be marked "done" standalone
   311→- Patches would confuse more than clarify
   312→
   313→```text
   314→                     ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
   315→                     │     Is this the same work?          │
   316→                     └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘
   317→                                    │
   318→                 ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
   319→                 │                  │                  │
   320→                 ▼                  ▼                  ▼
   321→          Same intent?      >50% overlap?      Can original
   322→          Same problem?     Same scope?        be "done" without
   323→                 │                  │          these changes?
   324→                 │                  │                  │
   325→       ┌────────┴────────┐  ┌──────┴──────┐   ┌───────┴───────┐
   326→       │                 │  │             │   │               │
   327→      YES               NO YES           NO  NO              YES
   328→       │                 │  │             │   │               │
   329→       ▼                 ▼  ▼             ▼   ▼               ▼
   330→    UPDATE            NEW  UPDATE       NEW  UPDATE          NEW
   331→```
   332→
   333→**Example: "Add dark mode"**
   334→
   335→- "Need to also support custom themes" → New change (scope exploded)
   336→- "System preference detection is harder than expected" → Update (same intent)
   337→- "Let's ship toggle first, add preferences later" → Update then archive, then new change
   338→
   339→## Best Practices
   340→
   341→### Keep Changes Focused
   342→
   343→One logical unit of work per change. If you're doing "add feature X and also refactor Y", consider two separate changes.
   344→
   345→**Why it matters:**
   346→- Easier to review and understand
   347→- Cleaner archive history
   348→- Can ship independently
   349→- Simpler rollback if needed
   350→
   351→### Use `/opsx:explore` for Unclear Requirements
   352→
   353→Before committing to a change, explore the problem space:
   354→
   355→```text
   356→You: /opsx:explore
   357→
   358→AI:  What would you like to explore?
   359→
   360→You: How should we handle rate limiting for our API?
   361→
   362→AI:  Let me investigate your current setup and options...
   363→     [Researches codebase, considers patterns]
   364→
   365→     I see three approaches we could take:
   366→     1. Middleware-based (simple, per-endpoint)
   367→     2. Redis-backed (scalable, shared state)
   368→     3. Token bucket per user (fair, complex)
   369→
   370→     Your current stack suggests #1 or #2. What's your scale?
   371→```
   372→
   373→Exploration clarifies thinking before you create artifacts.
   374→
   375→### Verify Before Archiving
   376→
   377→Use `/opsx:verify` to check implementation matches artifacts:
   378→
   379→```text
   380→You: /opsx:verify
   381→
   382→AI:  Verifying add-dark-mode...
   383→
   384→     ✓ All tasks in tasks.md are checked
   385→     ✓ Requirements in specs have corresponding code
   386→     ✓ Design decisions are reflected in implementation
   387→
   388→     Ready to archive!
   389→```
   390→
   391→Catches mismatches before you close out the change.
   392→
   393→### Name Changes Clearly
   394→
   395→Good names make `openspec list` useful:
   396→
   397→```text
   398→Good:                          Avoid:
   399→add-dark-mode                  feature-1
   400→fix-login-redirect             update
   401→optimize-product-query         changes
   402→implement-2fa                  wip
   403→```
   404→
   405→## Command Quick Reference
   406→
   407→For full command details and options, see [Commands](commands.md).
   408→
   409→| Command | Purpose | When to Use |
   410→|---------|---------|-------------|
   411→| `/opsx:explore` | Think through ideas | Unclear requirements, investigation |
   412→| `/opsx:new` | Start a change | Beginning any new work |
   413→| `/opsx:continue` | Create next artifact | Step-by-step artifact creation |
   414→| `/opsx:ff` | Create all planning artifacts | Clear scope, ready to build |
   415→| `/opsx:apply` | Implement tasks | Ready to write code |
   416→| `/opsx:verify` | Validate implementation | Before archiving, catch mismatches |
   417→| `/opsx:sync` | Merge delta specs | Optional—archive prompts if needed |
   418→| `/opsx:archive` | Complete the change | All work finished |
   419→| `/opsx:bulk-archive` | Archive multiple changes | Parallel work, batch completion |
   420→

## OpenSpec: Detailed Technical Analysis

### 1. What the Project Does Overall

OpenSpec is an **AI-native spec-driven development framework** -- a Node.js/TypeScript CLI tool and agent skill system that adds a structured specification layer on top of AI coding assistants. Its core purpose is to solve the problem of AI coding assistants being unpredictable when requirements live only in chat history.

The system provides:
- A structured **change management workflow** where each proposed change gets its own folder containing planning artifacts (proposal, specs, design, tasks)
- **Delta-based specifications** that describe what is changing relative to existing behavior (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED requirements), making it brownfield-friendly
- **Slash command integration** with 20+ AI tools (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, etc.) via generated skill files
- A **dependency-aware artifact graph** that determines which documents can be created at any point
- An **archive system** that merges delta specs into a main spec "source of truth" when changes are completed

### 2. How It Generates proposal.md, spec.md, design.md, tasks.md, and Other Markdown Files

**Critically, OpenSpec does NOT directly generate the markdown content itself.** Instead, it operates as an **orchestration and instruction layer** that tells AI assistants what to create and how. The actual markdown generation is performed by the AI assistant (Claude, GPT, etc.) following OpenSpec's structured instructions.

Here is the detailed mechanism:

#### A. Schema Definition (`/schemas/spec-driven/schema.yaml`)

The schema YAML file defines the artifact pipeline as a dependency graph:

```yaml
artifacts:
  - id: proposal
    generates: proposal.md
    template: proposal.md        # Points to templates/proposal.md
    instruction: |               # Detailed AI guidance for content
      Create the proposal document that establishes WHY this change is needed.
      Sections: Why, What Changes, Capabilities, Impact...
    requires: []                 # No dependencies - can be created first

  - id: specs
    generates: "specs/**/*.md"
    template: spec.md
    instruction: |
      Create specification files that define WHAT the system should do...
    requires: [proposal]         # Must have proposal first

  - id: design
    generates: design.md
    template: design.md
    instruction: |
      Create the design document that explains HOW to implement...
    requires: [proposal]

  - id: tasks
    generates: tasks.md
    template: tasks.md
    instruction: |
      Create the task list that breaks down the implementation work...
    requires: [specs, design]    # Needs both specs and design
```

#### B. Templates (`/schemas/spec-driven/templates/`)

Each artifact has a corresponding markdown template that provides the **skeleton structure**. For example, `templates/proposal.md`:

```markdown
## Why
<!-- Explain the motivation for this change. -->

## What Changes
<!-- Describe what will change. -->

## Capabilities
### New Capabilities
- `<name>`: <description>
### Modified Capabilities
- `<existing-name>`: <what is changing>

## Impact
<!-- Affected code, APIs, dependencies -->
```

These are minimal scaffolds with HTML comments as placeholders. The AI fills them in.

#### C. Instruction Generation Pipeline

When an AI assistant invokes a skill like `/opsx:continue` or `/opsx:ff`, the following code path executes:

1. **`loadChangeContext()`** (`src/core/artifact-graph/instruction-loader.ts`) loads the schema, builds the `ArtifactGraph`, and detects which artifacts are already completed by checking file existence on disk via `detectCompleted()`.

2. **`generateInstructions()`** (`src/core/artifact-graph/instruction-loader.ts`) produces an `ArtifactInstructions` object containing:
   - `changeName` and `artifactId`
   - `outputPath` -- where to write the file (e.g., `proposal.md`)
   - `instruction` -- the detailed guidance from the schema YAML
   - `template` -- the skeleton markdown from the templates directory
   - `context` -- optional project-level context from `openspec/config.yaml`
   - `rules` -- optional per-artifact constraints from config
   - `dependencies` -- which prerequisite artifacts exist and their file paths
   - `unlocks` -- which artifacts become available after this one

3. **`printInstructionsText()`** (`src/commands/workflow/instructions.ts`) formats everything into structured XML-like output that the AI consumes:

```xml
<artifact id="proposal" change="add-dark-mode" schema="spec-driven">

<task>
Create the proposal artifact for change "add-dark-mode".
Initial proposal document outlining the change
</task>

<dependencies>
Read these files for context before creating this artifact:
<dependency id="..." status="done">
  <path>/project/openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/proposal.md</path>
</dependency>
</dependencies>

<output>
Write to: /project/openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/specs/auth/spec.md
</output>

<instruction>
Create specification files that define WHAT the system should do...
</instruction>

<template>
## ADDED Requirements
### Requirement: <!-- requirement name -->
...
</template>

<unlocks>
Completing this artifact enables: design, tasks
</unlocks>

</artifact>
```

The AI assistant reads this structured output, reads the dependency files for context, and writes the markdown file to the specified output path.

### 3. The Workflow/Pipeline for Markdown Generation

The end-to-end workflow involves these stages:

**Stage 1: Initialization** (`openspec init`)
- `InitCommand` in `src/core/init.ts` creates the `openspec/` directory structure
- Generates **skill files** (SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter) for each configured AI tool
- Generates **slash command files** via tool-specific adapters (Claude, Cursor, etc.)
- Creates `openspec/config.yaml` with the default schema (`spec-driven`)

**Stage 2: New Change** (`/opsx:new <name>` or `openspec new change <name>`)
- `createChange()` in `src/utils/change-utils.ts` creates `openspec/changes/<name>/`
- Writes `.openspec.yaml` metadata file with schema name and creation date
- No markdown files are created yet

**Stage 3: Artifact Creation** (`/opsx:continue` or `/opsx:ff`)
- The skill instructs the AI to run `openspec instructions <artifact-id> --change <name> --json`
- The CLI calls `instructionsCommand()` which calls `generateInstructions()`
- The `ArtifactGraph` computes which artifacts are ready (all dependencies satisfied)
- For `/opsx:ff` (fast-forward), the AI iterates through the entire build order: `proposal` -> `specs` -> `design` -> `tasks`
- For `/opsx:continue`, only the next ready artifact is processed
- The AI reads the instructions, reads dependency files, and **writes the markdown file** to the output path

**Stage 4: Apply** (`/opsx:apply`)
- `generateApplyInstructions()` checks that required artifacts exist
- Parses `tasks.md` to extract checkbox items and their completion status
- The AI implements each task and marks checkboxes as `[x]` in `tasks.md`

**Stage 5: Archive** (`/opsx:archive`)
- `ArchiveCommand` in `src/core/archive.ts` merges delta specs into main specs
- The markdown parser (`src/core/parsers/markdown-parser.ts`) and change parser (`src/core/parsers/change-parser.ts`) parse delta specs
- ADDED requirements are appended, MODIFIED requirements replace existing ones, REMOVED requirements are deleted
- The change folder moves to `openspec/changes/archive/<date>-<name>/`

### 4. Key Code Files Involved in the Generation Process

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `/schemas/spec-driven/schema.yaml` | Defines the 4-artifact pipeline (proposal -> specs -> design -> tasks) with instructions and dependency ordering |
| `/schemas/spec-driven/templates/*.md` | Skeleton markdown templates for each artifact type |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/types.ts` | Zod schemas defining `Artifact`, `SchemaYaml`, `ApplyPhase` types |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/schema.ts` | Loads and validates schema YAML files; checks for duplicate IDs, invalid references, and cycles |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/graph.ts` | `ArtifactGraph` class -- computes topological build order (Kahn's algorithm), determines ready/blocked artifacts |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/state.ts` | `detectCompleted()` -- checks which artifacts exist by scanning the filesystem for generated files (supports glob patterns) |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/resolver.ts` | 3-tier schema resolution: project-local -> user override -> package built-in |
| `/src/core/artifact-graph/instruction-loader.ts` | `generateInstructions()` -- the central function that assembles instructions, templates, dependencies, context, and rules into `ArtifactInstructions` |
| `/src/commands/workflow/instructions.ts` | CLI command that formats instructions into XML-structured output for AI consumption; also handles apply instructions with task parsing |
| `/src/commands/workflow/new-change.ts` | Creates new change directories |
| `/src/utils/change-utils.ts` | `createChange()` -- validates name, resolves schema, creates directory and metadata |
| `/src/core/templates/skill-templates.ts` | Templates for all 10 agent skills (explore, new, continue, apply, ff, sync, archive, bulk-archive, verify, onboard) |
| `/src/core/shared/skill-generation.ts` | `generateSkillContent()` -- produces SKILL.md files with YAML frontmatter |
| `/src/core/init.ts` | `InitCommand` -- orchestrates setup: directory creation, skill generation, command generation |
| `/src/core/parsers/markdown-parser.ts` | Parses structured markdown (requirements, scenarios) for validation and archiving |
| `/src/core/parsers/change-parser.ts` | Parses delta spec sections (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED) for archive merging |
| `/src/core/archive.ts` | Archive logic -- merges deltas into main specs and moves change to archive |
| `/src/core/project-config.ts` | Reads `openspec/config.yaml` for project-level context and per-artifact rules |
| `/src/cli/index.ts` | CLI entry point using Commander.js -- registers all commands |

### Summary of the Key Insight

OpenSpec is fundamentally an **instruction orchestrator**, not a document generator. It:
1. Defines a DAG (directed acyclic graph) of artifacts via YAML schemas
2. Tracks completion state by checking filesystem for generated files
3. Produces richly structured instructions (with templates, guidance, dependencies, context) that AI assistants consume
4. The AI assistants do the actual markdown writing
5. After implementation, the archive system programmatically merges delta specs back into the main specification files
